content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fa:< (916) 322-0886 
Ms. Ina K. Bendis 
Governing Board Member 
Santa Clara Unified School District 
3 129 Barkley A venue 
Santa Clara, CA 95051 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-032 
Dear Ms. Bendis: 
March 10, 2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the .. Act").1 
QUESTION 
May you use campaign funds to pay for your membership dues of $71 to join the 
International Society for Philosophical Enquiry ("ISPE"), a Minnesota-based nonprofit 
organization whose membership is restricted to individuals of high intelligence as measured by 
standardized tests? 
CONCLUSION 
You stated your membership in lSPE is related to your service on the Santa Clara Unified 
School District Board because ISPE emphasizes educational achievement and public service and 
you will use your membership to discuss with other members issues about improving public 
education. Under the Act, you may use campaign funds for the payment of ISPE membership 
dues because the payment is reasonably related to a governmental purpose. 
FACTS 
You provicted the following facts in your request for advice. You serve as a governing 
board member of the Santa Clara Unified School District ("SCUSD"). Your political campaign 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 810001hrough 9IOl•t All statutory 
references are 10 the Government Code. unless otherwise indkated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections IR I 10 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless olherwise indicated. 


File No. A-11-032 
Page No. 2 
account is "Bendis for School Board 2010," committee identification No. 1289597. You ask 
whether you may use campaign funds to pay the initiation fee of $21 and first year membership 
dues of $50 to join ISPE. ISPE is a Minnesota-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization whose 
membership is restricted to individuals whose Intelligence Quotient (IQ) is at or above the 99.9th 
percentile, which you are qualified to join. 
You paid the initiation fee and membership dues on a credit card that you have n"ot used 
for any personal or campaign purpose during the current billing cycle. You had planned to pay 
the bill from your campaign account, but are inquiring with the FPPC to see if this expenditure of 
campaign funds is permissible. 
You provide the following reasons why ISPE membership relates to your service on the 
SCUSD Board of Education: 
1. ISPE particularly recognizes academic achievement and public service. You state that 
unlike most other high-IQ organizations, ISPE provides various levels of advancement that 
specifically relate to earning additional academic degrees as well as public service. 
2. Your candidacy for office on SCUSD's Board particularly related to your unusual 
level of intelligence and academic achievement. You state that one reason you ran for school 
board is that you believe unusually intelligent people make unusually correct analyses of 
complex challenging situations and unusually good decisions based on available data. This 
message was part of your campaign and resonated with many supporting donors and voters. 
Related to your unusual intellect, you add that you have an unusually robust education with three 
Doctoral level degrees (Ph.D., M.D., J.D.) and one Masters level (Stanford Graduate School of 
Business) degree. You have used this expertise to teach at high school, college and graduate 
levels. In your view and the view of many donors and voters, these educational achievements 
direct! y relate to your robust understanding of 21st century educational challenges and issues, and 
what we need to do to improve public education. You believe that ISPE's nexus tying 
educational achievement and public service to its membership levels connects ISPE membership 
to your work as a school board member .. 
3. You have already used, and will continue to use your ISPE membership to forward 
your work as a school board member. The most important benefit of ISPE membership for you 
is members' ability to access others of similarly high intelligence to discuss issues of interest to 
them by participating in ISPE's Yahoo Group. You have already received informative responses 
from other ISPE members with interest, knowledge and expertise in education, to specific 
requests you made there for input regarding public education. You anticipate receiving more 
input of this nature from fellow ISPE members. 
4. Other benefits of ISPE membership are peripheral. The benefits of ISPE membership 
are summarized on its website, http://www.thcthousand.com. They primarily consist of 
participatory access to ISPE's Yahoo Group (see above), subscription to ISPE's publication 
Telicom, and ability to submit articles to Telicom, which you anticipate doing in the context of 


File No. A-11-032 
Page No. 3 
public education issues. You are not aware of any other "personal" benefits from ISPE 
membership. 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Refonn Act's provisions concerning the pennissible uses of campaign funds 
are found in Sections 89510-89522. Under these provisions, campaign funds are regarded as 
separate and distinct from a candidate or officeholder's personal funds. The Act's campaign 
funds provisions are designed to ensure that campaign funds are used for electioneering and 
officeholder purposes, and are not expended for the candidate or officeholder's personal 
purposes. 
Section 89510 provides that all "contributions deposited into the campaign account shall 
be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate or for 
expenses associated with holding office." An expenditure associated with seeking or holding 
office is within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510 if it is reasonably 
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. Expenditures that confer a substantial 
personal benefit on an official must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental 
purpose. (Section 89512.) A "substantial personal benefit" means: "an expenditure of 
campaign funds which results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than two hundred 
dollars ($200) to a candidate, elected officer, or any individual or individuals with authority to 
approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee." (Section 8951 l(b)(3).) 
While the payment of membership dues to organizations is not expressly set forth in the 
use of campaign funds statutes, we have advised that such expenditures are pennitted so long as 
they are directly (or reasonably) related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. If the 
cost of the membership is less than $200, the expenditure must be reasonably related to a 
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Zimmer Advice Letter, No. 1-92-617.) 
The Commission has allowed the payment of membership fees with campaign funds in 
the past. The Kaloogian Advice Letter, No. 97-111, concluded that a legislator may use 
campaign funds or officeholder account funds to pay for the $50 annual membership dues to join 
the American Legislative Exchange Council and to attend ALEC' s annual convention, because 
the payment of these expenses is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose. The 
Garcetti Advice Letter. No. A-93-042, concluded that the Los Angeles District Attorney may use 
campaign funds to pay membership dues for local bar associations. The Eckel Advice Letter, 
No. A-91-488 concluded that a local official may use campaign funds to pay membership dues to 
the chamber of commerce. And the Winger Advice Letter, No. 1-97-185, advised that a county 
sheriff may use campaign funds or pfficeholder account funds for service club memberships to 
the Lions Club, Rotary Club, Kiwanis Club, Sons of Italy and the Italian Catholic Federation. 
You provided infonnation showing that your membership in ISPE is reasonably related to 
your service on the SCUSD board because ISPE emphasizes educational achievement and public 
service and you will use your membership to discuss with other members issues about public 


File No. A-11-032 
Page No. 4 
education and to enhance your understanding of 21st century educational challenges and what we 
need to do to improve public education. Because the payment of your membership dues in ISPE 
is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose, under the Act, you may use 
campaign funds to pay the $71 dues. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HPW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Scott Hallabrin 
General Counsel 
# f, i,J 
By: 
Hyla P. Wagner 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
